title: Sleep and Productivity
summary: A narrator explains how sleep quality affects cognitive performance, decision-making, and long-term health, and why modern working culture often ignores the science of rest.
body: For much of the twentieth century, sleeping fewer hours was quietly treated as a virtue. Ambitious professionals would boast about surviving on five or six hours a night, as though the ability to function on little sleep was proof of toughness and dedication. That attitude has not disappeared entirely, but a growing body of research is making it increasingly difficult to defend. Sleep, it turns out, is not simply a passive state in which the body and mind switch off. It is an active process during which the brain consolidates memories, clears out waste products, and restores the chemical balance needed for clear thinking. When we sleep well, we wake up better equipped to concentrate, solve problems, and regulate our emotions. When we sleep poorly, those same abilities begin to suffer in ways that are often invisible to us at the time. One of the most striking findings in sleep research is how bad people are at recognising their own impairment. Studies consistently show that individuals who are chronically sleep-deprived tend to believe they are performing normally, even when objective tests reveal significant declines in reaction time, memory accuracy, and judgement. This creates a dangerous illusion: people push themselves harder, convinced they are fine, while their performance quietly deteriorates. The relationship between sleep and productivity is therefore more complex than it first appears. Getting less sleep to fit in more working hours frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect. Tired employees make more errors, take longer to complete tasks, and struggle to think creatively. By contrast, workers who prioritise sleep consistently demonstrate better focus, stronger communication, and greater problem-solving ability. The financial cost of sleep deprivation to businesses is, by some estimates, enormous. Researchers at the RAND Corporation have calculated that the United States alone loses hundreds of billions of dollars each year due to reduced productivity caused by insufficient sleep. The science also points to serious long-term health consequences. Chronic sleep deprivation is associated with an increased risk of heart disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and certain mental health conditions. The evidence is strong enough that some health professionals now argue sleep should be treated as a public health priority rather than a personal lifestyle choice. So what can be done? Experts broadly agree on a few key principles. Adults need between seven and nine hours of sleep per night for optimal functioning. Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule — going to bed and waking at the same time every day — is one of the most effective strategies. Reducing screen exposure in the hour before bed and keeping the bedroom cool, dark, and quiet also makes a measurable difference. Small changes, made consistently, can significantly improve sleep quality over time.

annotations.meaningVi: được xem là đức tính tốt, điều đáng tự hào
annotations.exampleEn: Hard work has long been treated as a virtue in many cultures.

annotations.meaningVi: khoe khoang về, tự hào thái quá về
annotations.exampleEn: He always boasts about how quickly he can finish his work.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày càng nhiều nghiên cứu, khối lượng nghiên cứu ngày càng lớn
annotations.exampleEn: There is a growing body of research on the effects of social media on teenagers.

annotations.meaningVi: củng cố ký ức, lưu trữ ký ức lâu dài
annotations.exampleEn: Sleep helps the brain consolidate the information you learned during the day.

annotations.meaningVi: điều tiết cảm xúc, kiểm soát cảm xúc
annotations.exampleEn: Exercise can help you regulate your emotions after a stressful day.
People who are chronically sleep-deprived often struggle to focus at work.

annotations.meaningVi: các bài kiểm tra khách quan, đo lường không thiên lệch
annotations.exampleEn: Objective tests showed that his reaction time had slowed significantly.
Believing you can multitask effectively is often a dangerous illusion.

annotations.meaningVi: suy giảm, trở nên tệ hơn
annotations.exampleEn: His concentration deteriorates after working for more than four hours without a break.

annotations.meaningVi: ưu tiên, xếp hạng quan trọng hơn
annotations.exampleEn: You should prioritise sleep if you want to perform well the next day.
Sleep deprivation can impair judgement as severely as alcohol.

annotations.meaningVi: liên quan đến, có mối liên hệ với
annotations.exampleEn: Smoking is associated with an increased risk of lung disease.

annotations.meaningVi: ưu tiên sức khỏe cộng đồng
annotations.exampleEn: Reducing obesity has been named a public health priority by the government.

annotations.meaningVi: hoạt động tối ưu, hoạt động ở mức tốt nhất
annotations.exampleEn: A balanced diet supports optimal functioning of the immune system.

annotations.meaningVi: lịch ngủ đều đặn, giờ ngủ và thức cố định
annotations.exampleEn: Keeping a consistent sleep schedule helps your body clock stay in rhythm.

annotations.meaningVi: sự khác biệt có thể đo lường được, hiệu quả rõ ràng
annotations.exampleEn: Regular exercise makes a measurable difference to your cardiovascular health.

grammarNotes.title: Passive Voice — Scientific and Formal Writing
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Văn phong khoa học và học thuật thường dùng bị động để trình bày kết quả nghiên cứu một cách khách quan, không nhấn mạnh người thực hiện. Ví dụ: 'Sleep should be treated as a public health priority', 'Sleep deprivation is associated with an increased risk'. Cấu trúc: be + past participle. Đây là dấu hiệu phổ biến của văn phong học thuật mà học viên B1 cần nhận ra.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Scientific and academic writing frequently uses passive voice to present findings objectively, without emphasising who performed the research. Examples: 'Sleep should be treated as a public health priority', 'sleep deprivation is associated with'. Structure: be + past participle. Recognising this pattern is key for B1 learners reading or listening to academic content.

grammarNotes.title: Adverbial Phrases — Qualifying Statements
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài sử dụng nhiều trạng ngữ để giới hạn hoặc nhấn mạnh mức độ của một tuyên bố: 'often invisible to us', 'consistently show', 'significantly improve'. Các trạng từ này rất quan trọng vì chúng thể hiện sắc thái — một sự kiện không xảy ra tuyệt đối mà ở mức độ nào đó. Ở B1, học viên cần phân biệt 'often' (thường), 'consistently' (nhất quán, lần nào cũng vậy), và 'significantly' (đáng kể).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses adverbial phrases to qualify and intensify statements: 'often invisible to us', 'consistently show', 'significantly improve'. These adverbs are important because they signal degree — something does not happen absolutely but to a certain extent. B1 learners should distinguish between 'often' (frequently), 'consistently' (reliably, every time), and 'significantly' (to a notable degree).

translationVi:
Trong phần lớn thế kỷ hai mươi, ngủ ít giờ hơn được ngầm coi là một đức tính. Những người có chí tiến thủ thường khoe về việc chỉ ngủ năm hay sáu tiếng mỗi đêm, như thể khả năng hoạt động với ít giấc ngủ là bằng chứng của sự cứng rắn và cống hiến. Thái độ đó chưa hoàn toàn biến mất, nhưng ngày càng có nhiều nghiên cứu khiến việc bảo vệ quan điểm đó ngày càng khó khăn hơn.

Giấc ngủ, hóa ra, không đơn giản là trạng thái thụ động trong đó cơ thể và tâm trí tắt nguồn. Đó là một quá trình tích cực mà trong đó não củng cố ký ức, loại bỏ các chất thải, và khôi phục sự cân bằng hóa học cần thiết để tư duy rõ ràng. Khi chúng ta ngủ đủ giấc, chúng ta thức dậy được trang bị tốt hơn để tập trung, giải quyết vấn đề và điều tiết cảm xúc. Khi chúng ta ngủ kém, những khả năng tương tự đó bắt đầu suy giảm theo những cách thường vô hình với chúng ta vào thời điểm đó.

Một trong những phát hiện đáng chú ý nhất trong nghiên cứu về giấc ngủ là mức độ kém cỏi của mọi người khi nhận ra sự suy giảm của bản thân. Các nghiên cứu liên tục chỉ ra rằng những người bị thiếu ngủ mãn tính thường tin rằng họ đang hoạt động bình thường, ngay cả khi các bài kiểm tra khách quan tiết lộ sự suy giảm đáng kể về thời gian phản xạ, độ chính xác của trí nhớ và khả năng phán đoán. Điều này tạo ra một ảo tưởng nguy hiểm: mọi người tự đẩy mình làm việc nhiều hơn, tin rằng mình ổn, trong khi hiệu suất của họ đang âm thầm suy giảm.

Do đó, mối quan hệ giữa giấc ngủ và năng suất phức tạp hơn vẻ ngoài ban đầu. Ngủ ít hơn để có thêm giờ làm việc thường tạo ra kết quả ngược lại so với mục đích. Nhân viên mệt mỏi mắc nhiều lỗi hơn, mất nhiều thời gian hơn để hoàn thành công việc và khó suy nghĩ sáng tạo. Ngược lại, những người lao động ưu tiên giấc ngủ liên tục thể hiện sự tập trung tốt hơn, giao tiếp mạnh mẽ hơn và khả năng giải quyết vấn đề lớn hơn.

Chi phí tài chính của tình trạng thiếu ngủ đối với các doanh nghiệp, theo một số ước tính, là rất lớn. Các nhà nghiên cứu tại Tập đoàn RAND đã tính toán rằng riêng Hoa Kỳ mất hàng trăm tỷ đô la mỗi năm do năng suất giảm sút gây ra bởi giấc ngủ không đủ giấc.

Khoa học cũng chỉ ra những hậu quả sức khỏe lâu dài nghiêm trọng. Thiếu ngủ mãn tính liên quan đến nguy cơ tăng cao về bệnh tim, đái tháo đường type 2, béo phì và một số tình trạng sức khỏe tâm thần. Bằng chứng đủ mạnh để một số chuyên gia y tế hiện nay lập luận rằng giấc ngủ nên được coi là ưu tiên sức khỏe cộng đồng hơn là lựa chọn lối sống cá nhân.

Vậy chúng ta có thể làm gì? Các chuyên gia nhìn chung đồng ý về một số nguyên tắc chính. Người lớn cần từ bảy đến chín tiếng ngủ mỗi đêm để hoạt động tối ưu. Duy trì lịch ngủ đều đặn — đi ngủ và thức dậy cùng giờ mỗi ngày — là một trong những chiến lược hiệu quả nhất. Giảm tiếp xúc màn hình trong giờ trước khi ngủ và giữ phòng ngủ mát, tối và yên tĩnh cũng tạo ra sự khác biệt có thể đo lường được. Những thay đổi nhỏ, được thực hiện đều đặn, có thể cải thiện đáng kể chất lượng giấc ngủ theo thời gian.

questions.prompt: How was sleeping fewer hours viewed by ambitious professionals in the twentieth century?
options: As a health risk to be avoided
As a virtue and proof of dedication
As a temporary measure during busy periods
questions.explanation: The narrator says sleeping fewer hours 'was quietly treated as a virtue' and 'proof of toughness and dedication.'
questions.hint: Think about how the narrator describes the attitude toward short sleep.

questions.prompt: According to the narrator, what does the brain do during sleep?
options: It completely shuts down and rests
It replays the day's events without purpose
It consolidates memories, removes waste, and restores chemical balance
It prepares the body for physical activity the next day
questions.explanation: The narrator explains sleep is 'an active process during which the brain consolidates memories, clears out waste products, and restores the chemical balance needed for clear thinking.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes three specific functions that happen during sleep.

questions.prompt: What do studies show about sleep-deprived people's awareness of their own impairment?
options: They overestimate how tired they feel
They accurately identify their performance decline
They tend to believe they are performing normally despite significant decline
They report feeling confused and unable to work
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'individuals who are chronically sleep-deprived tend to believe they are performing normally, even when objective tests reveal significant declines.'
questions.hint: The narrator calls this a 'dangerous illusion.'

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say about getting less sleep to work more hours?
options: It is effective if done only occasionally
It frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect
It helps workers meet short-term deadlines
It is acceptable as long as weekends are used for recovery
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'Getting less sleep to fit in more working hours frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect.'
questions.hint: Think about what happens to tired workers' productivity.

questions.prompt: How do workers who prioritise sleep perform differently from those who do not?
options: They tend to work longer hours overall
They make fewer social connections at work
They show better focus, communication, and problem-solving ability
They are promoted more quickly due to good behaviour
questions.explanation: The narrator says workers who prioritise sleep 'consistently demonstrate better focus, stronger communication, and greater problem-solving ability.'

questions.prompt: What organisation conducted research on the economic cost of sleep deprivation in the US?
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'Researchers at the RAND Corporation have calculated that the United States alone loses hundreds of billions of dollars each year.'
questions.hint: The narrator names the organisation directly.

questions.prompt: Which health conditions are associated with chronic sleep deprivation according to the narrator?
options: Heart disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and some mental health conditions
High blood pressure, migraines, and poor eyesight
Kidney disease, liver disease, and reduced immunity
questions.explanation: The narrator lists: 'heart disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and certain mental health conditions.'
questions.hint: The narrator lists four specific conditions.

questions.prompt: How many hours of sleep do experts say adults need per night for optimal functioning?
questions.explanation: The narrator states: 'Adults need between seven and nine hours of sleep per night for optimal functioning.'
questions.hint: The narrator gives a specific range.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator describe as one of the most effective sleep strategies?
options: Taking a long nap in the afternoon
Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule every day
Using a sleep tracking app to monitor patterns
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule — going to bed and waking at the same time every day — is one of the most effective strategies.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes it as going to bed and waking at the same time.

questions.prompt: What environmental factors does the narrator recommend for better sleep?
options: A warm, humid room with minimal noise
A cool, dark, and quiet bedroom
White noise machines and thick curtains only
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'keeping the bedroom cool, dark, and quiet also makes a measurable difference.'
questions.hint: Three adjectives describe the ideal bedroom environment.

questions.prompt: What argument do some health professionals now make about sleep?
options: That sleep should be taught as a subject in schools
That employers should enforce mandatory rest periods
That sleep should be treated as a public health priority, not just a personal choice
That sleeping pills should be more widely available
questions.explanation: The narrator says: 'some health professionals now argue sleep should be treated as a public health priority rather than a personal lifestyle choice.'
questions.hint: They contrast a public responsibility with an individual one.

questions.prompt: What is the 'dangerous illusion' the narrator refers to?
options: Believing that naps can fully replace night-time sleep
Thinking that sleep problems always have a medical cause
Believing you are performing well when sleep deprivation has already impaired you
Assuming that working fewer hours leads to lower productivity
questions.explanation: The narrator explains: 'people push themselves harder, convinced they are fine, while their performance quietly deteriorates.'
questions.hint: The illusion is about self-awareness — or the lack of it.

cloze.template: For much of the twentieth century, sleeping fewer hours was quietly treated as a {{b1}}. Ambitious professionals would boast about surviving on five or six hours a night, as though the ability to function on little sleep was proof of toughness and dedication. That attitude has not disappeared entirely, but a growing body of research is making it increasingly difficult to defend. Sleep, it turns out, is not simply a passive state in which the body and mind switch off. It is an active process during which the brain {{b2}} memories, clears out waste products, and restores the chemical balance needed for clear thinking. When we sleep well, we wake up better equipped to concentrate, solve problems, and regulate our {{b3}}. When we sleep poorly, those same abilities begin to suffer in ways that are often invisible to us at the time. One of the most striking findings in sleep research is how bad people are at recognising their own impairment. Studies consistently show that individuals who are {{b4}} sleep-deprived tend to believe they are performing normally, even when objective tests reveal significant declines in reaction time, memory accuracy, and judgement. This creates a dangerous {{b5}}: people push themselves harder, convinced they are fine, while their performance quietly {{b6}}. The relationship between sleep and productivity is therefore more complex than it first appears. Getting less sleep to fit in more working hours frequently produces the opposite of the intended effect. Tired employees make more errors, take longer to complete tasks, and struggle to think creatively. By contrast, workers who {{b7}} sleep consistently demonstrate better focus, stronger communication, and greater problem-solving ability. The financial cost of sleep deprivation to businesses is, by some estimates, enormous. Researchers at the RAND Corporation have calculated that the United States alone loses hundreds of billions of dollars each year due to reduced productivity caused by {{b8}} sleep. The science also points to serious long-term health consequences. Chronic sleep deprivation is {{b9}} with an increased risk of heart disease, type 2 diabetes, obesity, and certain mental health conditions. Experts broadly agree that adults need between seven and nine hours of sleep per night for {{b10}} functioning. Maintaining a consistent sleep schedule — going to bed and waking at the same time every day — is one of the most effective strategies.

blanks.explanation: 'Treated as a virtue' means it was considered a morally good quality — the narrator contrasts this with the scientific evidence that followed.
'Consolidates memories' is the specific scientific term used — it means the brain strengthens and organises memories during sleep.
'Regulate our emotions' means to manage and control emotional responses — this is a key benefit of sufficient sleep.
'Chronically sleep-deprived' means suffering from persistent, long-term sleep loss — 'chronically' is a medical term for something ongoing.
'Dangerous illusion' is the narrator's exact phrase — it describes the false belief that one is performing well despite being sleep-deprived.
'Deteriorates' is the verb used in the passage — it means to become progressively worse over time, especially through neglect.
'Prioritise sleep' means to treat sleep as a high priority, ensuring it is not sacrificed for other activities.
'Insufficient sleep' means not enough sleep — 'insufficient' is a formal adjective meaning below the required amount.
'Associated with' is a standard academic collocation meaning there is a statistical or scientific relationship between two things.
'Optimal functioning' means operating at the best possible level — 'optimal' is a formal adjective meaning the most favourable or best.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator argues that modern working culture undervalues sleep, even though the science strongly supports its importance for performance and health. Why do you think this attitude persists despite the evidence? What changes — in workplaces, schools, or society — would be needed to genuinely prioritise sleep?
